corpus_building.implemented_as: The TXT paper is converted into a section-aware annotated mini-corpus.

tokenization.implemented_as: Every concept phrase and sentence is normalized into tokens for counts and n-grams.

normalization.implemented_as: Lower-casing and punctuation cleanup are used for phrase matching and deduplication.

n_gram.implemented_as: Concept length and document top unigrams/bigrams/trigrams are exposed.

bow_and_naive_bayes.implemented_as: Section BoW hits act as class-conditional section evidence for intro/method/experiment/conclusion style decisions.

tf_idf_document_term_matrix.implemented_as: Document-level frequency and TF-IDF matrices expose which section concepts are characteristic of this paper.

laplace_smoothing.implemented_as: P(section | concept) uses add-one smoothing, so unseen section-concept pairs are not forced to zero.

evidence_cue_lexicon.implemented_as: Cue phrases such as propose, achieve, outperform, and BLEU give transparent rule-based evidence hints.

sequence_labeling.implemented_as: The first model contributes BIO boundary evidence for concept phrase extraction.

canonicalization_noisy_channel_style.implemented_as: Observed surface phrases are mapped to canonical terms with an alias confidence score.

location.sentence: We present Momentum Contrast (MoCo) as a way of building large and consistent dictionaries for unsupervised learning with a contrastive loss (Figure 1).

context_window.previous: However, ex- isting methods that use contrastive losses can be limited in one of these two aspects (discussed later in context).
context_window.current: We present Momentum Contrast (MoCo) as a way of building large and consistent dictionaries for unsupervised learning with a contrastive loss (Figure 1).
context_window.next: We maintain the dictionary as a queue of data samples: the encoded repre- sentations of the current mini-batch are enqueued, and the oldest are dequeued.

location.sentence: We propose a momentum update to address this issue.

location.sentence: This paper’s focus is on a mechanism for general con- trastive learning; we do not explore orthogonal factors (such as specific pretext tasks) that may further improve accuracy.

context_window.previous: By using an architec- ture that is not customized for the pretext task, it is easier to transfer features to a variety of visual tasks and make com- parisons, studied in the next subsection.
context_window.current: This paper’s focus is on a mechanism for general con- trastive learning; we do not explore orthogonal factors (such as specific pretext tasks) that may further improve accuracy.
context_window.next: As an example, “MoCo v2” [8], an extension of a prelim- inary version of this manuscript, achieves 71.1% accuracy with R50 (up from 60.6%), given small changes on the data augmentation and output projection head [7].

location.sentence: The dictionary keys { k 0 , k 1 , k 2 , ... } are defined on-the-fly by a set of data samples.

context_window.previous: Momentum Contrast (MoCo) trains a visual represen- tation encoder by matching an encoded query q to a dictionary of encoded keys using a contrastive loss.
context_window.current: The dictionary keys { k 0 , k 1 , k 2 , ... } are defined on-the-fly by a set of data samples.
context_window.next: The dictionary is built as a queue, with the current mini-batch en- queued and the oldest mini-batch dequeued, decoupling it from the mini-batch size.

location.sentence: With similarity measured by dot product, a form of a contrastive loss function, called InfoNCE [46], is consid- ered in this paper: L q = − log exp( q · k + /τ ) ∑ K i =0 exp( q · k i /τ ) (1) where τ is a temperature hyper-parameter per [61].

top_candidates.sentence: This dataset is well-balanced in its class distribution, and its images generally contain iconic view of objects.
Instagram-1B (IG-1B): Following , this is a dataset of ∼1 billion (940M) public images from Instagram.
This dataset is relatively uncurated comparing to IN-1M, and has a long-tailed, unbalanced distribution of real-world data.
This dataset contains both iconic objects and scene-level images.
MoCo can outperform its supervised pre-training counterpart in 7 detection/segmentation tasks on PASCAL VOC, COCO, and other datasets, sometimes surpassing it by large margins.
Several recent studies present promising results on unsupervised visual representation learning using approaches related to the contrastive loss .
We present Momentum Contrast (MoCo) as a way of building large and consistent dictionaries for unsupervised learning with a contrastive loss (Figure ).
Using this pretext task, MoCo shows competitive results under the common protocol of linear classification in the ImageNet dataset .

In these experiments, we explore MoCo pre-trained on Ima-geNet or on a one-billion Instagram image set, demonstrating that MoCo can work well in a more real-world, billionimage scale, and relatively uncurated scenario.
For IN-1M, we use a mini-batch size of 256 (N in Algorithm 1) in 8 GPUs, and an initial learning rate of 0.03.
We present Momentum Contrast (MoCo) for unsupervised visual representation learning.

downstream_contract.intended_use: Feed this JSON to a later local open-source model to write an overview.
